title: EU Locks In Free Cabin Bag Rule for Flights, Effective 2027
excerpt: The EU Council gave final approval to new air passenger rules guaranteeing a free cabin bag and clearer fares - but the changes won't apply until second half of 2027.
content:
Europe's decade-long fight over cabin bag fees is finally over - at least on paper. On July 13, the Council of the EU gave final legal clearance to a sweeping overhaul of air passenger rights, following the European Parliament's 646-12 vote in favor on July 7. The reform guarantees every passenger a free personal item and standard cabin bag, forces airlines to show that cost upfront in the headline fare, and tweaks the compensation rules travelers have relied on since 2004. For anyone booking flights to or from Europe, here is what actually changes, and, just as importantly, when.

The headline change is baggage. Every passenger flying with an EU carrier, or departing from an EU airport, will be entitled to a free personal item measuring roughly 40 by 30 by 15 centimeters - think a small backpack or laptop bag - plus a standard cabin bag, without a separate charge. Airlines can still sell cheaper fares to passengers who opt out of the larger cabin bag, so budget carriers keep some pricing flexibility. What changes is transparency: booking sites and airlines must display the fare including that baggage allowance from the very first screen of the checkout flow, rather than tacking on a bag fee later in the process. The rules also extend to fee-free name corrections on tickets, digital boarding passes that do not require a mandatory app, and nine months (up from the current shorter window in many countries) to file a delay or cancellation compensation claim.

Compensation amounts themselves are largely preserved rather than expanded. Payouts stay in the familiar 250-600 euro range, with 300 euros applying to delays on flights over 3,500 kilometers and up to 600 euros for cancellations or delays beyond four hours. Airlines still get 30 days to pay out or formally invoke extraordinary circumstances. Consumer groups had pushed for stronger delay thresholds; what passed largely keeps today's EU261 framework intact while closing loopholes around transparency and fees.

Ryanair, unsurprisingly, is not celebrating. Michael O'Leary called the reform bureaucratic bunkum and argued it will effectively force airlines to advertise higher headline prices, since roughly half of Ryanair's passengers currently choose to fly without a second cabin bag and benefit from cheaper base fares. O'Leary has cited an estimated 8 euro impact on average ticket prices once bag costs are folded into displayed fares. Whether that plays out as a real fare increase or simply a more honest starting price is likely to be argued over well into 2027.

Here is the part that matters most for anyone booking a trip today: nothing changes yet. The regulation enters into force 12 months and 20 days after it is published in the EU's Official Journal, which puts real-world implementation in the second half of 2027. Today's EU261 rules, including current baggage fee structures on budget carriers, remain fully in effect for the rest of 2026 and well into next year. If you are booking flights to Spain, Italy, Greece or anywhere else in the EU this summer or for a 2027 trip before the new rules land, expect the same bag fees and add-on pricing you see today from Ryanair, easyJet, and Wizz Air.

What should travelers actually do with this news? First, don't delay a booking waiting for free bags - that entitlement is roughly a year away and won't retroactively apply to tickets bought now. Second, keep comparing fares the way you already do: check whether a listed budget fare includes a cabin bag, since today's booking engines still show bag-free base prices as the headline number. Third, if you're a frequent flyer to Europe, expect fare structures to shift gradually through 2027 as airlines adjust pricing ahead of the mandatory transparency requirement - watch for budget carriers restructuring bundles well before the legal deadline, since airlines rarely wait until the last moment to reprice. Finally, hold onto your existing rights: the current 250-600 euro compensation regime for delays and cancellations is unaffected and still enforceable today, so keep documentation of any disrupted EU flight regardless of when this new law eventually takes effect.
